context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this corruption reflection tbh. idk how to connect the “grease the wheel” thing, Hong Kong, and state legitimacy all in one piece.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's start simple before trying to connect everything. In your own words, what do you think corruption actually signals about a state? Like, what does it tell you when corruption is widespread in a government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their thinking process and feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them or explaining the connections, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about one core concept first - what corruption actually means in relation to the state. This will naturally lead toward the other parts. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them do the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: idk, like maybe the state is weak? but also not always? ngl that’s where i get confused rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful instinct. But think about this: can a strong, powerful state also be corrupt? What would that tell you about the difference between a state's *power* and its *legitimacy*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good intuitive observation (weak state) but is already sensing its limitations, which is great critical thinking. Rather than validating or correcting directly, I should push them to interrogate their own idea further with a Socratic question. The goal is to get them toward the concept of legitimacy, which is central to the prompt.

exchanges.student: wait yeah maybe it can, and that’s kinda messing me up lol. i don’t really get how power and legitimacy are different here.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: a government can force you to do something through threat or violence - that's power. But why do people sometimes *voluntarily* follow rules or pay taxes without being forced? What would you call that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely grappling with the power/legitimacy distinction, which is a key conceptual hurdle for this assignment. I should ask a concrete question to help them discover the difference themselves rather than explaining it. A real-world example framing might help them arrive at the distinction on their own.

exchanges.student: uhh legitimacy i think, but then idk how corruption fits bc ppl still follow stuff even if officials are corrupt fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good push-back. So if people still follow rules despite corruption, what do you think happens to that willingness over time? Does it stay the same, or does something shift?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they've grasped the basic legitimacy concept and are now pushing back with a nuanced observation. This is good critical thinking. I should acknowledge this briefly and use a Socratic question to push them toward thinking about what happens to legitimacy over time when corruption persists, and how that connects to the 'grease the wheel' thesis and development outcomes.

exchanges.student: maybe it drops over time? tbh i’m still super fuzzy and i kinda need the bigger picture of the whole prompt.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually building the bigger picture piece by piece - trust it. Now, the 'grease the wheel' thesis basically says corruption can *help* development in some contexts. Does that idea sit comfortably with what you just said about legitimacy eroding over time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing some frustration and asking for the bigger picture. They've been building good conceptual ground (power vs legitimacy, erosion of trust). Rather than giving them a roadmap or overview, I should acknowledge their progress and redirect them to the 'grease the wheel' part of the prompt using a Socratic question, since that's the next logical piece to tackle. This keeps the conversation moving forward without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: not really, and that’s why i’m stuck lol. can u just explain how those ideas fit together bc rn it all feels contradictory.
exchanges.tutor: I won't resolve it for you - but here's a hint: that contradiction you're feeling? That's not a problem to fix, that might actually be the central argument of your paper. So let me ask: is Hong Kong a case that supports or undermines the 'grease the wheel' thesis, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the connection directly, which I should refuse. However, they are genuinely stuck on a real tension in the material, which is actually the productive intellectual core of the assignment. I should validate that the tension is real and intentional, then use a Socratic question to help them think through it rather than resolving it for them. The contradiction they're sensing IS the argument they need to make in their paper.

exchanges.student: i honestly can’t tell, bc if hong kong did well by reducing corruption then doesn’t that just kill the thesis?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation. So if Hong Kong succeeded by *eliminating* corruption, what does that suggest about the states that use the 'grease the wheel' argument to justify it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong logical move - using Hong Kong as a counter-example to the 'grease the wheel' thesis. This is good critical thinking. I should affirm the direction without confirming the full conclusion, and push them to think about what the Hong Kong case specifically tells us about the relationship between anti-corruption efforts, state capacity, and development success. This will help them build toward a more nuanced argument.

exchanges.student: maybe that they’re using it as an excuse? idk, but then i’m worried that sounds too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: It's a strong instinct - now complicate it yourself. What conditions allowed Hong Kong to actually root out corruption? Does every state have those same conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical instinct - they've arrived at a compelling idea (corruption as justification/excuse) but are self-doubting. Rather than reassuring them or elaborating on the idea, I should push them to complexify it themselves by connecting it back to state legitimacy and the conditions that make anti-corruption reform possible or impossible. This will add nuance to their thinking without me doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: yeah see this is where i spiral again, bc i feel like now i need a whole lecture on hong kong, corruption theory, and state-building tbh.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have more than enough to start writing. You've got: corruption eroding legitimacy over time, the 'grease the wheel' thesis as a possible justification, and Hong Kong as a counter-example. That's the spine of a reflection piece. Why not try writing a rough opening paragraph pulling those ideas together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and feeling like they need more background knowledge before they can write. This is a common avoidance pattern. We've actually covered enough conceptual ground for them to attempt a draft - they have ideas about legitimacy erosion, the 'grease the wheel' thesis as potential justification, and Hong Kong as a counter-example. I should redirect them to start writing and integrate what they've already worked out, rather than going deeper into research rabbit holes.

exchanges.student: i can’t really rn bc i still don’t know what my actual position is supposed to be. can u just give me the main possible positions ppl take on this prompt?
exchanges.tutor: You've already been staking out a position throughout our conversation. Look back at what you said about legitimacy eroding, and corruption being used as an excuse. What does that add up to for you - is corruption-free development possible or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially outline the possible answers for them, which I should refuse as it would bypass their own reasoning process. However, they've actually already developed a position through our conversation without realizing it. I should point them back to their own statements and push them to recognize that they've already been building an argument.
